exemplar.title: To what extent does an individualâs ecological footprint and economic status affect the environmental value systems in Saudi Arabia?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does an individualâs ecological footprint and economic status affect the environmental value systems in Saudi Arabia?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report structure follows IB ESS IA with clear headings and a table of contents
Correct use of key ESS terminology
weaknesses: Section numbering and figure placement are inconsistent
Occasional spelling/terminology errors and verbose phrasing
Bibliography format is inconsistent and incomplete
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure, terminology and coherence mostly meet the 2-mark descriptors but fall short of fully sustained organization and concise, error-free presentation required for 3 marks.

strengths: Clear, focused research question specifying independent and dependent variables with geographical scope
weaknesses: Environmental context lacks depth, quantitative data and socio-economic detail
Connections between EVS definitions and environmental literacy lack theoretical support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research question is highly focused and coherent (band 5â6), but the treatment of the environmental issue and the theoretical connection between variables remain at a descriptive level (both band 3â4). A holistic mark of 4 reflects this mix of high performance in formulation and mid-level performance in contextualization and linkage.

strengths: Method clearly described and repeatable by another researcher
Sampling strategy pros and cons outlined
Ethical considerations (anonymity, voluntary participation) are addressed
weaknesses: No sample size justification or detailed coding rules for ties
Convenience sampling justification is brief and lacks discussion of bias impact
Risk assessment lacks a formal matrix or detailed protocol
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands sit in the 3â4 band: the method is repeatable but missing sufficiency details, sampling is described but not fully justified, and risk/ethical considerations are outlined but not comprehensive. A score of 3 accurately reflects this consistent mid-band performance.

strengths: Relates conclusions to existing Saudi Arabian survey data
Identifies key method strengths, weaknesses and a primary limitation
Proposes meaningful improvements to sampling and questionnaire design
weaknesses: Evaluation is brief and omits broader socio-cultural implications
Does not critique assumptions of the ecological footprint tool
Improvement suggestions lack specific design details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand demonstrates descriptive evaluation and practical suggestions fitting the 3â4 band. The treatment is solid but incomplete, meriting 3 marks overall.

strengths: Comprehensive raw data tables and clearly labelled graphs
Trend descriptions are correct and free of major errors
weaknesses: Graphs omit error bars and some relevant data plots
No statistical tests or measures of uncertainty are applied
Conclusion is based on descriptive trends without statistical support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis are accurate yet incomplete (band 3â4), and conclusions are valid but lack robust support. A holistic mark of 3 captures the correct but partial execution across all three strands.

strengths: Proposes a logical application (environmental education for children) tied to behaviour change
Discusses strengths and weaknesses of the proposed solution
weaknesses: Justification is loosely linked to the IA findings
Evaluation omits considerations of cost and cultural barriers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application is described with some justification and partial evaluation of strengths/weaknesses, aligning clearly with the 2-mark band.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS terms such as âecological footprintâ, âEVSâ and âconvenience samplingâ are used correctly, yet the prose is occasionally verbose and includes spelling errors (âconvienceâ, âeccentricâ instead of âecocentricâ).  Usage is appropriate but concision inconsistent, fitting the 2-mark descriptor.

The student relates the studyâs conclusions to a published Saudi Arabian survey and comments on discrepancies between values and actions, giving some environmental context.  The evaluation, however, is brief and omits broader societal implications, fitting the middle of the 3-4 band.

A concluding statement directly answers the research question and is based on observed patterns, but support is limited to descriptive comparisons without statistical significance.  This aligns with âinterprets trends â¦ with some validityâ, securing a 3 in the 3-4 band.

The student calculates averages and qualitatively comments on trends but undertakes no statistical testing and does not quantify uncertainty.  The analysis is generally correct but incomplete, matching the 3-4 descriptor.

The student proposes switching to a more representative sampling method and refining economic questions.  These modifications are meaningful but not extensive, and no specific further research design is outlined, corresponding to the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

The method (online questionnaire divided into EF, economic status and EVS sections) is described clearly enough that another researcher could repeat it, but several details needed for sufficiencyâsample size justification, coding rules for ties, statistical testsâare absent.  Thus it is repeatable but not entirely appropriate for collecting *sufficient* data, placing it in the 3-4 band.

The student explains that ecological footprint and economic status may influence an individualâs EVS, briefly defining ecocentric, technocentric and anthropocentric positions.  The link between these concepts and the overall issue of environmental literacy is stated but some causal pathways and theoretical support are missing, so the explanation remains partial.  This aligns with âdescribes connections â¦ with some omissionsâ, meriting 3-4 rather than the comprehensive 5-6.

The student formulates a single, explicit question that specifies both independent factors (ecological footprint and economic status), the dependent construct (environmental value system) and the geographical scope (Saudi Arabia).  The wording is grammatically clear and narrow enough to be investigated with the stated questionnaire method, demonstrating coherence and focus.  Minor ambiguity about how two independent variables will be disentangled prevents a perfect score but the strand still fits securely in the 5-6 band.

Strengths (long-lasting impact) and weaknesses/limitations (resource inequality, delayed effect) are discussed, but not all relevant factorsâcosts, cultural barriersâare evaluated.  This partial evaluation fits the 2-mark band.

The report follows the IB ESS IA structure with clear headings and a table of contents, but internal section numbering is inconsistent and some tables/figures are embedded awkwardly.  Organisation is therefore present but not sustained flawlessly, matching 2 marks.

Strengths, weaknesses and a key limitation of convenience sampling are identified and discussed, but several methodological issues (question validity, EF calculator assumptions) are not addressed.  The coverage is therefore descriptive with omissions, warranting a 3.

The student outlines environmental literacy in Saudi Arabia, cites one published survey and briefly explains the potential future impact of low literacy.  Although relevant, the treatment is descriptive rather than analytical and lacks quantitative evidence or deeper socio-economic context.  Consequently the strand meets the âoutlinesâ descriptor with some contextual detail, but does not reach the sustained discussion required for 5-6.

The student names convenience sampling and outlines its pros and cons, but offers only a brief, pragmatic justification (âdifficulty getting responsesâ).  There is no exploration of alternative strategies or sampling error, so the work âdescribesâ rather than âjustifiesâ the choice, fitting the 3-4 band with a mid-band score.

Risk and ethics are discussed (anonymity, voluntary participation, potential bias).  The treatment is adequate but not detailedâno formal risk table or ethical consent protocol is provided.  This constitutes an outline rather than a comprehensive description, matching the 3-4 band.

All raw data are tabulated and two graphs are produced; axes are labelled and titles present.  However, the graphs display only averaged EF and response counts, omitting error bars or economic-status-versus-EF plots, so some relevant data remain unvisualised.  This satisfies âconstructs appropriate diagrams â¦ with some omissionsâ (3-4 band).

A single solutionâenhanced environmental education for childrenâis explained with reference to long-term behavioural change, giving some justification.  The rationale is only loosely linked to the IA findings and remains general, placing it in the 2-mark band.

The argument generally proceeds logically from method to results to discussion, but some leapsâe.g., concluding misalignment of values from average EF without statistical supportâinterrupt coherence.  Thus the work is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, meriting 2 marks.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear and focused, specifying independent and dependent variables with geographical scope. Consider clarifying how ecological footprint and economic status will be disentangled analytically.
The environmental issue is stated but lacks depth. Expand context with quantitative literacy rates or socio-economic factors in Saudi Arabia to strengthen the background.
Avoid restating the research question verbatim in the introduction. Instead, briefly summarize its rationale and link to the issue.
The discussion of environmental literacy cites one survey but remains descriptive. Integrate additional data or critique the methodology of the cited study to deepen analysis.
The connection between EVS definitions and environmental literacy is outlined but lacks theoretical support. Cite an ESS model or framework to justify these links.
âUncontrolled Variablesâ section mislabels factors that are actually controlled. Refine terminology and distinguish clear controls from uncontrolled influences.
Categorization of economic status by a âdominatingâ category is vague. Define a scoring rubric or index to assign high, medium, low status rigorously.
The method design mentions questionnaire sections but omits sample size justification and coding rules for ties. Add these details to ensure sufficiency of data collection.
The removal of a question led to classification ties. Pilot the survey to detect ambiguous items and ensure an odd number of valid EVS questions.
Convenience sampling justification notes ease but omits discussion of demographic bias. Explain how this affects generalizability.
Ethical section lists anonymity and bias but lacks a formal risk assessment matrix. Include a table categorizing risks, likelihood and mitigation.
Raw data table is exhaustiveâgood inclusionâbut overwhelming. Summarize key statistics (means, medians) in a separate table for clarity.
Graphs are clearly labeled but omit error bars or confidence intervals. Incorporate measures of variability to convey uncertainty.
Graph axes lack unit labels (e.g. âAverage EF (gha)â and âCount of EVSâ). Ensure both axes are titled with units for reader comprehension.
Analysis correctly describes trends but omits statistical testing. Consider a chi-square test or ANOVA to support significance of observed patterns.
The term âeccentricâ appears instead of âecocentricâ in data description. Ensure correct terminology to maintain precision.
Interpretation uses vague phrases like âa lot of resourcesâ. Replace with quantitative descriptors (e.g., % increase) to enhance rigor.
Conclusion restates findings but repeats âvalues don't align with their valuesâ. Revise phrasing for clarity and logical coherence.
Evaluation relates to one published survey but omits broader socio-cultural implications in Saudi Arabia. Discuss wider societal impacts.
Method evaluation notes sampling bias but overlooks validity of EF calculator assumptions. Critically assess accuracy of the footprint tool.
Improvement suggestion to adopt unbiased sampling is valid but lacks specifics. Propose a stratified or cluster sampling design with target quotas.
Application proposal to educate children is logical but weakly justified against your data. Link specific findings to educational strategies.
Evaluation of the solution omits cost and cultural barriers. Include analysis of resource requirements and social acceptance.
Consider outlining a pilot environmental education program with metrics (e.g., pre/post-literacy tests) to evaluate effectiveness.
Bibliography uses inconsistent citation formats and lacks DOI information. Standardize to a recognized style (e.g., APA) and include full publication details.
